title: Collections Utility Class
description: The java.util.Collections helper class -- fully explaining Collections.sort() from the Comparable vs Comparator lesson, plus other handy static methods.
objectives: Understand Collections as a utility class of static helper methods for collections
Use sort(), reverse(), max(), min(), and shuffle()
Distinguish the Collections utility class from the Collection/Collections Framework concept

sections.text: We've used Collections.sort(students) and Collections.sort(students, byName) since Comparable vs Comparator without naming Collections itself. It's a utility class -- a class made entirely of static helper methods (recall Static vs Instance Methods), none of which need an object to call.
A class in java.util providing static helper methods that operate on List, Set, and other collections -- sorting, searching, reversing, and more.

Collections (capital C, the utility class) is easy to confuse with Collection (the base interface List/Set both extend) -- they sound nearly identical but serve completely different roles. Collections is a toolbox of static methods; Collection is an interface type.

Integer already implements Comparable<Integer> (numeric order), so Collections.sort(numbers) works directly without needing a custom Comparator -- exactly like String's natural alphabetical order used in earlier lessons.

sections.title: Common Collections methods
rows: Reverses the list's current order, in place

sections.text: Collections.reverse(numbers) here just reverses whatever order numbers was ALREADY in ([30, 10, 20] became [20, 10, 30]) -- it does NOT sort first. To get descending sorted order, you'd sort() first, then reverse(), or use a Comparator that reverses the comparison.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: max(scores) still correctly finds 92 regardless of the list's current order, since it scans the whole collection

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing Collections (the utility class) with Collection (the base interface) -- similarly named but entirely different roles.
Assuming Collections.reverse() sorts the list -- it only reverses whatever order already exists.
Calling Collections.sort() on a list of a custom class with no Comparable implementation and no Comparator argument -- this fails to compile, as covered in Comparable vs Comparator.

Forgetting these are STATIC methods, called as Collections.sort(...), never on a specific list instance like list.sort_() (note: List does have its own instance sort(comparator) method too, but Collections.sort predates it and remains extremely common).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the Collections class?
questions.answer: A utility class in java.util providing static helper methods for operating on collections -- such as sort(), reverse(), max(), min(), and shuffle() -- none of which require creating an object to call.

questions.question: What is the difference between Collections and Collection?
questions.answer: Collections (with an s) is the static utility class of helper methods. Collection (no s) is the base interface that List, Set, and Queue all extend -- they are unrelated despite the similar name.

questions.question: Does Collections.reverse() sort a list?
questions.answer: No. It only reverses whatever order the list currently has -- to get descending sorted order, you'd typically sort() first and then reverse(), or supply a Comparator that reverses the comparison directly.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Collections is a utility class in java.util offering static helper methods for collections: sort() (using Comparable or a supplied Comparator), reverse() (flips current order, doesn't sort), max()/min() (find extremes), and shuffle() (randomizes order). It's easily confused with Collection, the unrelated base interface that List and Set extend. A common combo is sort() followed by reverse() to get descending order, since reverse() alone only flips whatever order already exists rather than sorting from scratch.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Collections (with an s) = the TOOLBOX of static helpers. Collection (no s) = the INTERFACE family (List, Set, Queue). reverse() flips what's there -- it does NOT sort for you.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is the Collections utility class used for?
What's the difference between Collections and Collection?
Does Collections.reverse() sort a list, or just flip its current order?
How would you get a list sorted in descending order using Collections?
